title: CRITICAL: agentic init's controlled bd-init can un-commit unrelated pushed work
description: Discovered 2026-07-23 during the ~/automation bd cutover. agentic/initialize.py's _controlled_bd_init() runs bd init then unconditionally 'git reset HEAD~1' to undo bd's own auto-commit, assuming bd init's commit is always exactly the new HEAD. In ~/automation this assumption was false: bd init's commit landed on top of an already-pushed, unrelated commit (64b7d5b, 'docs: close manual-fix open items as won't-fix'), and the reset silently un-committed THAT real work back into the working tree (still present, not lost, but uncommitted and at risk of being discarded by any subsequent hard-reset/checkout). The worker caught it via reflog and it was reconciled cleanly during a push rebase this time -- but the mechanism is a live landmine: any repo where something else commits between the start of agentic init and its internal bd-init call (a concurrent process, a pre-commit hook, bd's own hooks writing a commit first) risks this same silent un-commit, and a future run might not get caught/recovered as luckily. Fix: _controlled_bd_init must verify the commit it's about to reset IS bd's own auto-commit (e.g. check the commit message/author matches bd's known pattern, or capture the exact SHA bd's init call produces and reset only if HEAD still equals that SHA) before resetting, and abort loudly instead of resetting blind if that check fails.

title: design: decouple the session-refresh guardrail from agentprof's cost-attribution machinery
description: Investigated 2026-07-23 after a hypothesis that agentprof's p90_ctx was folding subagent tokens into main-session context (RULED OUT: internal/costsummary/costsummary.go structurally excludes agent-stack samples; TestBuildSessionsSectionContextPercentilesMainLoopOnly covers exactly this case). Two separate open questions surfaced during that investigation, maintainer-raised: (1) is cache_read_tokens+input_tokens per model call actually the right proxy for what degrades attention/costs a re-prime, or just a convenient one -- worth scrutiny, not verified deeply; (2) should hooks/session-refresh/refresh-check.sh keep depending on agentprof's --since-windowed cost-summary machinery at all, versus a simpler, more direct read of the CURRENT session's own live context size (context size and token-use-for-cost-attribution are different though related problems -- agentprof should stay the deep tool for the latter; the hook just needs 'a reasonable guardrail' for the former, cheaply and reliably). Candidate outcome: a simpler/more direct context-size source for the hook specifically, decoupled from agentprof's session-summary computation, while agentprof keeps owning richer cost/cache/skill-attribution analysis.
close_reason: done: hooks/session-refresh/refresh-check.sh redesigned to read transcript_path directly off the hook's own stdin payload, no agentprof dependency (commit 2a563440); agentprof retained separately for general cost-attribution digging

title: drain/SKILL.md's 'Launch authorization' section may contradict CLAUDE.md's 'contract blocks are gone' claim
description: CLAUDE.md's authoring conventions say core task 11 retired the per-skill launch-authorization contract and 'the contract blocks are gone from the SKILL.md files.' But task 09 (which ran after 11, rewriting drain/SKILL.md 496->114 lines) left/reintroduced a paragraph headed '**Launch authorization (execution stage).**' in drain/SKILL.md (lines ~15-19). It's a short citation to .claude/rules/untrusted-data.md, not a restated full contract, so it may be fine as a brief pointer -- but it's worth a maintainer read to decide: trim to a bare citation with no heading, or is CLAUDE.md's 'gone from the SKILL.md files' claim itself now inaccurate and needs softening.
